result:
The smell of coffee is a lie. It promises alertness but delivers only the jitters, a racing heart and the irrational conviction that the third email of the morning is a life-or-death operation. Still, I brew it every day, watching the dark bloom spiral through water like cheap galaxy footage. Ritual masquerading as remedy.  

Outside, the neighbor’s hydrangeas have undertaken a coup, thrusting blue fists over the fence, declaring independence from soil. I root for them the way I root for any organism that refuses its assigned border: weeds in pavement, teenagers in hoodies, apostrophes that squat outside the grammar rulebook.  

My phone buzzes with headlines written by bots who have never bled. Somewhere a stock soars because a CEO tweeted a lone emoji; elsewhere a village slips into the sea, unremarked, like a comma deleted by an overzealous proofreader. The feed scrolls faster than grief can crystallize.  

Inside the house, the refrigerator hums a single note—B-flat, I think—steadier than any national anthem. I close my eyes and match my breathing to it. For ten seconds I am a drone resting on the hive, wings quiet, stomach full of future honey. Then the kettle clicks off, the spell breaks, and I pour another cup of the lying darkness, grateful that at least the steam, rising, resembles something like honest work.

Inside the house, the refrigerator hums a single note—B-flat, I think—steadier than any national anthem. I close my eyes and match my breathing to it. prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
